title: Audit Trail Review: A Practical Guide for GMP Systems
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: A practical, risk-based guide to audit trail review for GMP systems, aligned to Annex 11, MHRA data integrity guidance and ALCOA+ for UK and EU teams.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury pharmaceutical banner in a cool teal and cyan palette set against deep navy, with a single subtle green accent light. A gloved hand of a quality professional rests beside a sleek tablet and laptop screen displaying softly blurred rows of time-stamped data and review markers, shot with shallow depth of field so only the foreground stays crisp. Soft natural light rakes across brushed-steel laboratory surfaces and a faint double-helix motif etched in glass behind, evoking precision, trust and data integrity. Composition 4:3, cinematic, ultra-clean, no text, no logos.

faqs.q: How often should audit trails be reviewed?
faqs.a: Frequency should be driven by risk under ICH Q9 rather than a fixed calendar. For high-risk, GMP-critical data such as analytical results supporting batch release, the audit trail is best reviewed before the associated decision is made, ideally as part of the second-person review of the original record. Lower-risk system events may be reviewed on a defined periodic basis, provided the rationale is documented.

faqs.q: Who should perform an audit trail review?
faqs.a: The reviewer should be independent of the person who generated the data and competent in both the process and the computerised system. This independence is essential, as reviewing your own audit trail provides little genuine assurance. They must understand the system well enough to recognise anomalies such as reprocessing, deletions or out-of-sequence activity, and escalate them through your deviation and CAPA process.

faqs.q: What is the difference between an audit trail and an audit trail review?
faqs.a: An audit trail is the secure, computer-generated record of who did what and when within a system. An audit trail review is the active human evaluation of that record to detect errors, integrity weaknesses or falsification. The MHRA is clear that simply generating an audit trail is not sufficient; without meaningful review, regulators may treat the control as effectively absent.
